title: Today's Tech Brief: Wikipedia editors plot strike and banner sabotage after Wikimedia layoffs
summary: Wikipedia editors consider strike and sabotage after Wikimedia layoffs

body.text: I'm looking at a story from The Register about some significant changes at Wikimedia. Apparently, the organization behind Wikipedia has laid off the team responsible for many community-requested fixes and moderation tools. This move has sparked quite a reaction from Wikipedia editors. It's not every day you see a community rally around a team of employees, but I guess that's what happens when you mess with the behind-the-scenes folks who keep the wheels turning.

In response to the layoffs, Wikipedia editors are plotting a strike and some form of banner sabotage. It seems the editors are upset about the disbanding of the team that handled many of their requests. My read is that this is a clear sign of how much the community values the work that team did. It's like losing the office IT guy – you don't really appreciate them until they're gone. I wonder what kind of sabotage they're planning; hopefully, it won't involve any epic pranks.

Another interesting piece of news is that the laid-off team was responsible for many community-requested fixes and moderation tools. Without them, the platform's infrastructure might not be as robust. I imagine it's a bit like when your favorite restaurant loses its head chef – the menu might not be the same. On a related note, Reddit's r/netdev community is also dealing with some growing pains, and let's just say their mods are having a blast dealing with all the 'fun'.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.

sources.title: Wikipedia editors plot strike and banner sabotage after Wikimedia layoffs
